middleton-mayes.text: Mayes Middleton (Republican) is a candidate for Attorney General of Texas.

On immigration, Mayes Middleton's recorded position: Middleton's attorney general campaign platform pledges to sue to stop so-called 'sanctuary cities' from 'ignoring the law and harboring illegal criminals' and to 'aggressively enforce President Trump's border security agenda and deportation orders.'

Mayes Middleton's recorded background: Mayes Middleton is a Republican state senator representing Senate District 11 (elected 2022, serving since January 2023) who previously served two terms in the Texas House (2019-2023) chairing the Texas House Freedom Caucus; he is president of Middleton Oil Company, an independent oil and gas firm, and is the Republican nominee for Texas Attorney General in 2026.
As of 2026-05-16, Mayes Middleton's campaign had raised $5,719,372 in campaign receipts and spent $9,309,283, per Texas Ethics Commission filings (this filing period only, not a cycle-to-date total).

johnson-nathan.text: Nathan Johnson (Democratic) is a candidate for Attorney General of Texas.

On education, Nathan Johnson's recorded position: Johnson has said he would not defend Texas's law requiring the Ten Commandments be displayed in public school classrooms if elected attorney general, calling it unconstitutional: 'I will not defend the legislature's passage of a requirement that schools place the Ten Commandments in classrooms because it's unconstitutional.'

Nathan Johnson's recorded background: Nathan Johnson is a Democratic state senator representing Senate District 16 in Dallas County (since January 2019) and a business litigator at Thompson Coburn LLP; he is the Democratic nominee for Texas Attorney General in 2026 after winning a May 2026 primary runoff.
As of 2026-05-16, Nathan Johnson's campaign had raised $365,981 in campaign receipts and spent $442,785, per Texas Ethics Commission filings (this filing period only, not a cycle-to-date total).

oxford-tom.text: Tom Oxford (Libertarian) is a candidate for Attorney General of Texas.

Tom Oxford's recorded background: Tom Oxford is a Beaumont, Texas attorney who has practiced personal injury and immigration law with the Waldman Smallwood firm; running as the Libertarian Party's nominee, he previously ran unsuccessfully for the Texas Supreme Court, Place 3, in 2022.
As of 2026-06-30, Tom Oxford's campaign had raised $394 in campaign receipts and spent $0, per Texas Ethics Commission filings (this filing period only, not a cycle-to-date total).

base.summary: Attorney General of Texas race: Mayes Middleton, Nathan Johnson, Tom Oxford. This overview lists each candidate's party, background, and campaign finance where available in our data set.
base.caveats: Our data set for this race covers candidate name, party, incumbency, background summary, and campaign finance totals where a filing was found (federal candidates file with the FEC; Texas state offices file with the Texas Ethics Commission -- each finance bullet names which). We do not have recorded voting histories or issue-by-issue policy positions for these candidates in this data set.

now.text: On immigration, Mayes Middleton's recorded position is: "Middleton's attorney general campaign platform pledges to sue to stop so-called 'sanctuary cities' from 'ignoring the law and harboring illegal criminals' and to 'aggressively enforce President Trump's border security agenda and deportation orders.'" That's where Mayes Middleton stands right now, this term.

long_term.text: If Mayes Middleton's recorded position on immigration holds and becomes sustained policy, it would likely keep shaping this issue the same way over the next decade. This is a projection, not a guarantee.
long_term.assumption: Assumes Mayes Middleton keeps this position and it turns into lasting policy, rather than changing later or staying just talk.

middleton-mayes.text: On education, Mayes Middleton's recorded position: In a January 2025 Senate committee hearing on Senate Bill 2 (education savings accounts), Middleton spoke in support of the program and said he expects it to 'create opportunities for more private schools to open that specialize in providing special education services.'

On education, Mayes Middleton's recorded position: State Sen. Mayes Middleton authored Senate Bill 176 (2023), which would create education savings accounts letting participating families receive 'the average amount of money it costs Texas public schools to educate each of their children' -- about $10,000 a year -- to spend on private-school tuition, online schooling, tutoring, or higher-education costs.

As of 2026-05-16, Mayes Middleton's campaign had raised $5,719,372, per Texas Ethics Commission filings (this filing period only, not a cycle-to-date total) -- fundraising scale only, not a stance on property taxes or public school funding.

johnson-nathan.text: On education, Nathan Johnson's recorded position: Johnson has said he would not defend Texas's law requiring the Ten Commandments be displayed in public school classrooms if elected attorney general, calling it unconstitutional: 'I will not defend the legislature's passage of a requirement that schools place the Ten Commandments in classrooms because it's unconstitutional.'

On taxes, Nathan Johnson's recorded position: On the Legislature's property-tax-cut package, Johnson said it had 'too much frosting on the cupcake' and was 'too big' given it relied on a one-time budget surplus, but that faced with an up-or-down vote he supported it: 'It's either property tax reduction, or no property tax reduction. And in principle, I support the whole thing.'

As of 2026-05-16, Nathan Johnson's campaign had raised $365,981, per Texas Ethics Commission filings (this filing period only, not a cycle-to-date total) -- fundraising scale only, not a stance on property taxes or public school funding.

middleton-mayes.text: On healthcare, Mayes Middleton's recorded position: In a candidate questionnaire, Middleton selected the position that 'Medicaid and Medicare should remain available, but no other taxpayer-funded [healthcare] programs are necessary,' and said he opposes government medical databases over concerns about 'abuse and violations of privacy.'

As of 2026-05-16, Mayes Middleton's campaign had raised $5,719,372, per Texas Ethics Commission filings (this filing period only, not a cycle-to-date total) -- fundraising scale only, not a stance on health coverage, the ACA, or Medicaid.

johnson-nathan.text: On healthcare, Nathan Johnson's recorded position: Johnson's campaign site says that after seeing Texas's 'worst-in-the-nation uninsured rate,' he 'became the state's leading voice on Medicaid expansion' in the state Senate, and describes his work reforming 'the Texas marketplace to enable hundreds of thousands of Texans to buy private health insurance.'

As of 2026-05-16, Nathan Johnson's campaign had raised $365,981, per Texas Ethics Commission filings (this filing period only, not a cycle-to-date total) -- fundraising scale only, not a stance on health coverage, the ACA, or Medicaid.
